criterion: Quality and efficiency of the implementation

sub_sections.title: Work plan and resources
sub_sections.content:
OVERVIEW OF WORK PLAN STRUCTURE

The MAESTRO project is structured across nine work packages (WP1–WP9) organised in four functional layers across a 48-month research and innovation programme. This architecture directly satisfies the evaluator criterion for quality and effectiveness of the work plan: each layer has a defined purpose, a clear output chain, and measurable progress indicators aligned with the call requirement for KPIs, benchmarking, and progress monitoring (CC-06; SR-06).

Layer 1 — Project Governance: WP1 (Project Management and Coordination, Lead: ATU, M1–M48) provides continuous consortium oversight, quality assurance, ethics compliance management, and coordination with the ADRA partnership and GenAI4EU central Hub as required by SR-09, CC-03, and CC-04.

Layer 2 — Research Pillars (parallel): WP2 (Neuro-Symbolic Planning and Reasoning Engine, Lead: ATU, M1–M36) addresses robust planning and reasoning via hybrid neuro-symbolic design (SR-02); WP3 (Adaptive Memory Architecture, Lead: BIIS, M1–M30) addresses effective memory and state management for long-horizon agent interactions (SR-03); WP4 (Decentralised Multi-Agent Coordination, Lead: CERIA, M3–M36) delivers formally verified multi-agent coordination protocols (SR-05c). The three pillar WPs proceed in parallel from project start, with integration contracts locked at MS1 (M3) to ensure compatible interfaces throughout independent development — directly mitigating RISK-10 (integration failure).

Layer 3 — Sector Demonstrators (staged from M12): WP5 (Healthcare Demonstrator, Lead: NIHS, M12–M42), WP6 (Manufacturing Demonstrator, Lead: ISIA, M12–M42), and WP7 (Logistics Transfer Demonstrator, Lead: ELI, M12–M42) deploy and validate the MAESTRO framework in three application domains. The M12 start date for all three demonstrators is deliberate: pillar WPs have reached evaluated intermediate prototypes (MS2 and MS3 at M12; MS4 at M15) before integration into real-world deployment environments. Each demonstrator follows three internal phases: (1) use case and data specification (M12–M18); (2) MAESTRO framework integration and deployment (M18–M36); (3) validation and results analysis (M36–M42). Together WP5–WP7 address SR-07 (Apply AI sector demonstration) across healthcare and advanced manufacturing as primary sectors, and a cross-sector logistics transfer confirming architecture generalisation.

Layer 4 — Integration and Dissemination (horizontal): WP8 (Integration, Evaluation and Benchmarking, Lead: FIIT, M6–M48) begins at M6 to define the evaluation framework (T8-01) before demonstrator activities launch, ensuring all WPs operate under a shared measurement regime. WP8 subsequently integrates all three research pillars into the unified MAESTRO framework (T8-02, M18–M42) and produces the community benchmark release via the AI-on-demand platform (T8-03, M42–M48) per SR-10 and CC-05, including Testing and Experiment Facility validation per CC-07. WP9 (Dissemination, Exploitation and Communication, Lead: BAL, M1–M48) operates continuously; the dissemination and exploitation plan (D9-01) is delivered at M6 as required, and the open-source release (D9-02) at M36 enables community adoption during the final validation phase.

TABLE 3.1A — LIST OF WORK PACKAGES

WP No. | WP Title | Lead | Start Month | End Month
WP1 | Project Management and Coordination | ATU | M1 | M48
WP2 | Neuro-Symbolic Planning and Reasoning Engine | ATU | M1 | M36
WP3 | Adaptive Memory Architecture | BIIS | M1 | M30
WP4 | Decentralised Multi-Agent Coordination | CERIA | M3 | M36
WP5 | Healthcare Demonstrator — Clinical Decision Support | NIHS | M12 | M42
WP6 | Manufacturing Demonstrator — Process Optimisation | ISIA | M12 | M42
WP7 | Logistics Transfer Demonstrator — Cross-Sector Generalisation | ELI | M12 | M42
WP8 | Integration, Evaluation and Benchmarking | FIIT | M6 | M48
WP9 | Dissemination, Exploitation and Communication | BAL | M1 | M48

TABLE 3.1B — WORK PACKAGE DESCRIPTIONS

WP1 — Project Management and Coordination (Lead: ATU; Contributors: CERIA, BIIS, ISIA, NIHS, ELI, BAL, FIIT; M1–M48)
Objectives: (1) Ensure effective consortium governance, administrative management, and reporting. (2) Maintain quality assurance, risk management, and ethics compliance across all project activities.
Tasks: T1-01 Consortium governance and financial management (ATU, M1–M48): implementation of consortium agreement provisions, financial management, and preparation of periodic progress reports to the European Commission. T1-02 Quality assurance and risk monitoring (ATU, M1–M48): implementation of the quality plan (D1-01), continuous tracking of risk register entries, and activation of mitigation measures as required. T1-03 Ethics compliance oversight and coordination with GenAI4EU Hub (ATU, M1–M48): management of Categories 2, 4, and 8 ethics triggers, coordination with ADRA and GenAI4EU central Hub per SR-09, CC-03, CC-04.
Deliverables: D1-01 Project management and quality plan (Report, PU, M3); D1-02 Data management plan (DMP, PU, M6).
Dependencies: None (source WP).

WP2 — Neuro-Symbolic Planning and Reasoning Engine (Lead: ATU; Contributors: CERIA, BIIS, BAL; M1–M36)
Objectives: (1) Design and implement a hybrid neuro-symbolic planning engine with formal sub-task graph decomposition. (2) Integrate MCTS and HTN planning with LLM-based heuristics and verifiable plan self-correction.
Tasks: T2-01 Sub-task graph decomposition design with formal pre/post-conditions (ATU, M1–M12): formal architecture of the planning engine including sub-task graph structures, pre/post-conditions, and decomposition strategies forming the foundation for integrated MAESTRO planning. T2-02 MCTS and HTN planning integration with LLM heuristics (ATU, M6–M24): integration of Monte Carlo Tree Search and Hierarchical Task Network planning with LLM-based heuristics to produce the core hybrid reasoning capability. T2-03 Plan monitoring and self-correction implementation (ATU, M18–M36): runtime plan monitoring, deviation detection, and verifiable self-correction enabling robust autonomous operation in demonstrator environments.
Deliverables: D2-01 Neuro-symbolic planning architecture specification (Report, PU, M18); D2-02 Planning engine software prototype (Software, PU, M36).
Dependencies: D2-02 consumed by WP5, WP6, WP7, and WP8 as a core framework input.

WP3 — Adaptive Memory Architecture (Lead: BIIS; Contributors: ATU, CERIA, BAL; M1–M30)
Objectives: (1) Design and implement a unified tri-store memory architecture with a learned memory controller. (2) Develop memory indexing, retrieval, and forgetting mechanisms for long-horizon agent interactions.
Tasks: T3-01 Tri-store memory architecture design (BIIS, M1–M12): architectural specification of working memory (immediate context), episodic memory (interaction history), and semantic memory (structured knowledge) with a unified controller governing information flows across stores. T3-02 Memory indexing and retrieval strategy implementation (BIIS, M6–M24): implementation of indexing and retrieval algorithms optimised for agent interaction continuity across long-horizon tasks. T3-03 Forgetting mechanism and coherence maintenance (BIIS, M18–M30): principled forgetting preventing memory saturation and coherence-maintenance procedures ensuring consistency across memory stores.
Deliverables: D3-01 Memory architecture design specification (Report, PU, M12); D3-02 Adaptive memory system software (Software, PU, M30).
Dependencies: D3-02 consumed by WP5, WP6, WP7, and WP8.

WP4 — Decentralised Multi-Agent Coordination (Lead: CERIA; Contributors: ATU, BIIS; M3–M36)
Objectives: (1) Develop formally specified task-delegation and negotiation protocols for heterogeneous AI agents. (2) Demonstrate convergence guarantees and auditable multi-agent interaction with shared tool access governance.
Tasks: T4-01 Task-delegation and negotiation protocol design (CERIA, M3–M15): formal specification of delegation and negotiation protocols for heterogeneous agent ensembles with game-theoretic grounding. T4-02 Convergence and conflict resolution under partial observability (CERIA, M12–M24): convergence proofs for core task classes and conflict resolution mechanisms for realistic multi-agent scenarios with partial observability. T4-03 Auditable multi-agent interaction trace module (CERIA, M18–M36): auditable interaction traces supporting explainability, accountability, and regulatory compliance including EU AI Act high-risk system obligations.
Deliverables: D4-01 Multi-agent coordination protocol specification (Report, PU, M18); D4-02 Decentralised coordination software (Software, PU, M36).
Dependencies: D4-02 consumed by WP5, WP6, WP7, and WP8.

WP5 — Healthcare Demonstrator — Clinical Decision Support (Lead: NIHS; Contributors: ATU, BIIS, BAL, FIIT; M12–M42)
Objectives: (1) Validate the MAESTRO framework in a clinical decision support use case with HL7 FHIR integration. (2) Conduct clinical expert validation of specialist sub-agents against diagnostic benchmarks.
Tasks: T5-01 Clinical use case definition and diagnostic benchmark design (NIHS, M12–M18): specification of clinical decision support use cases and diagnostic benchmarks drawing on NIHS de-identified clinical datasets, with ethics approvals confirmed by MS5 (M12). T5-02 HL7 FHIR integration and specialist sub-agent deployment (NIHS, M18–M36): integration of MAESTRO agents with FHIR-compliant healthcare data infrastructure and deployment of specialist clinical sub-agents leveraging WP2 planning and WP3 memory capabilities. T5-03 Clinical expert validation and results analysis (NIHS, M36–M42): structured clinical expert validation on 500 clinical cases and quantitative performance analysis against diagnostic benchmarks.
Deliverables: D5-01 Clinical decision support use case specification (Report, SEN, M18); D5-02 Healthcare demonstrator evaluation dataset (Dataset, SEN, M42).
Dependencies: Requires WP2 (D2-02), WP3 (D3-02), WP4 (D4-02). D5-02 feeds WP8.

WP6 — Manufacturing Demonstrator — Process Optimisation (Lead: ISIA; Contributors: ATU, CERIA, BAL, FIIT; M12–M42)
Objectives: (1) Validate the MAESTRO framework for manufacturing process optimisation in a pilot factory setting. (2) Demonstrate multi-agent real-time coordination across production segments against defined KPIs.
Tasks: T6-01 Production scenario definition and KPI framework design (ISIA, M12–M18): definition of manufacturing process scenarios and quantitative KPIs in the ISIA Industry 4.0 pilot factory environment. T6-02 Digital twin integration and multi-agent coordination deployment (ISIA, M18–M36): integration of MAESTRO agents with the ISIA pilot factory digital twin and multi-agent deployment across production segments using WP4 coordination protocols. T6-03 Pilot factory operational validation (ISIA, M36–M42): 3-month operational validation of the manufacturing demonstrator under real production conditions.
Deliverables: D6-01 Manufacturing use case and KPI specification (Report, PU, M18); D6-02 Manufacturing process optimisation demonstrator software (Demonstrator, PU, M42).
Dependencies: Requires WP2, WP3, WP4. D6-02 feeds WP8.

WP7 — Logistics Transfer Demonstrator — Cross-Sector Generalisation (Lead: ELI; Contributors: CERIA, BAL, FIIT; M12–M42)
Objectives: (1) Validate MAESTRO generalisation in logistics and supply chain operations across three international corridors. (2) Confirm integrated architecture transferability beyond primary call sectors using real operational data.
Tasks: T7-01 Logistics scenario and disruption recovery benchmark design (ELI, M12–M24): design of supply chain disruption recovery scenarios across three ELI international operational corridors with associated benchmarks. T7-02 Agent-based planning and negotiation deployment across supply chain (ELI, M18–M36): deployment of MAESTRO planning and negotiation agents across ELI operational supply chains testing multi-actor coordination at scale. T7-03 Operational validation across three international corridors (ELI, M36–M42): full operational validation confirming architecture transferability from healthcare and manufacturing domains to logistics.
Deliverables: D7-01 Logistics demonstrator specification and benchmark report (Report, PU, M24); D7-02 Cross-sector generalisation evaluation dataset (Dataset, SEN, M42).
Dependencies: Requires WP2, WP3, WP4. D7-02 feeds WP8.

WP8 — Integration, Evaluation and Benchmarking (Lead: FIIT; Contributors: ATU, CERIA, BIIS, ISIA, NIHS, ELI, BAL; M6–M48)
Objectives: (1) Define a cross-cutting evaluation framework and integrate all MAESTRO research pillars into a unified system. (2) Produce a standardised benchmark suite for community adoption and cross-demonstrator evaluation.
Tasks: T8-01 Cross-cutting evaluation framework and KPI definition (FIIT, M6–M12): early establishment of the MAESTRO evaluation framework and KPI suite ensuring all WPs operate under a shared measurement regime consistent with CC-06 and SR-06. T8-02 MAESTRO unified framework integration (FIIT, M18–M42): integration of WP2 planning engine, WP3 memory architecture, and WP4 coordination protocols into the unified MAESTRO platform and validation across all three demonstrator environments. T8-03 Cross-demonstrator benchmarking and community benchmark release (FIIT, M42–M48): final cross-demonstrator evaluation and community release of the MAESTRO benchmark suite on the AI-on-demand platform per SR-10 and CC-05, with TEF validation per CC-07.
Deliverables: D8-01 Evaluation framework and benchmark specification (Report, PU, M12); D8-02 MAESTRO benchmark suite dataset (Dataset, PU, M42); D8-03 Final integration and evaluation report (Report, PU, M48).
Dependencies: Receives WP2, WP3, WP4, WP5, WP6, WP7 outputs. D8-03 feeds WP9.

WP9 — Dissemination, Exploitation and Communication (Lead: BAL; Contributors: ATU, ISIA, ELI, FIIT; M1–M48)
Objectives: (1) Manage open-source framework release, community building, and AI-on-demand platform contributions per SR-10 and CC-05. (2) Develop and execute the consortium exploitation strategy and lead public communication activities.
Tasks: T9-01 Open-source release management and community building (BAL, M18–M48): management of the MAESTRO open-source release on GitHub, documentation, tutorials, and community engagement including EDIH dissemination initiation in three or more hubs. T9-02 Exploitation strategy development and IP management (BAL, M3–M36): development of individual and joint exploitation pathways and IP management coordinated across all eight partners. T9-03 Communication, website, and public engagement (BAL, M1–M48): project website from M1, regular public communications, event participation, and outreach aligned with GenAI4EU Hub activities.
Deliverables: D9-01 Dissemination and exploitation plan (Report, PU, M6); D9-02 Open-source software release (Software, PU, M36); D9-03 Final dissemination and impact report (Report, PU, M48).
Dependencies: T9-01 and final open-source community release use D8-03 final evaluation results.

TABLE 3.1C — LIST OF DELIVERABLES

No. | Deliverable Name | WP | Lead | Type | Dissemination | Due Month
D1.1 | Project management and quality plan | WP1 | ATU | R | PU | M3
D1.2 | Data management plan | WP1 | ATU | DMP | PU | M6
D2.1 | Neuro-symbolic planning architecture specification | WP2 | ATU | R | PU | M18
D2.2 | Planning engine software prototype | WP2 | ATU | OTHER | PU | M36
D3.1 | Memory architecture design specification | WP3 | BIIS | R | PU | M12
D3.2 | Adaptive memory system software | WP3 | BIIS | OTHER | PU | M30
D4.1 | Multi-agent coordination protocol specification | WP4 | CERIA | R | PU | M18
D4.2 | Decentralised coordination software | WP4 | CERIA | OTHER | PU | M36
D5.1 | Clinical decision support use case specification | WP5 | NIHS | R | SEN | M18
D5.2 | Healthcare demonstrator evaluation dataset | WP5 | NIHS | DATA | SEN | M42
D6.1 | Manufacturing use case and KPI specification | WP6 | ISIA | R | PU | M18
D6.2 | Manufacturing process optimisation demonstrator software | WP6 | ISIA | DEM | PU | M42
D7.1 | Logistics demonstrator specification and benchmark report | WP7 | ELI | R | PU | M24
D7.2 | Cross-sector generalisation evaluation dataset | WP7 | ELI | DATA | SEN | M42
D8.1 | Evaluation framework and benchmark specification | WP8 | FIIT | R | PU | M12
D8.2 | MAESTRO benchmark suite dataset | WP8 | FIIT | DATA | PU | M42
D8.3 | Final integration and evaluation report | WP8 | FIIT | R | PU | M48
D9.1 | Dissemination and exploitation plan | WP9 | BAL | R | PU | M6
D9.2 | Open-source software release | WP9 | BAL | OTHER | PU | M36
D9.3 | Final dissemination and impact report | WP9 | BAL | R | PU | M48

Type codes: R=document/report; DMP=data management plan; OTHER=software/algorithms; DATA=datasets; DEM=demonstrator/pilot/prototype. Dissemination: PU=public fully open; SEN=sensitive limited under Grant Agreement. D1-02 (DMP) and D9-01 (dissemination and exploitation plan) are delivered within M6 as required.

TABLE 3.1D — LIST OF MILESTONES

MS No. | Milestone Name | WP | Due Month | Means of Verification
MS1 | Architecture specification and integration contracts finalised | WP1 | M3 | Shared data representations, API contracts, and integration interfaces for all three research pillars and the External Tool and API Orchestration Layer documented, reviewed, and accepted by all pillar leads. Tool registry schema and API adapter interface specifications finalised. Integration test harness operational.
MS2 | Planning engine v1.0 — baseline neuro-symbolic integration | WP2 | M12 | Planning engine decomposes five or more benchmark task types into sub-task graphs with verifiable pre/post-conditions. End-to-end evaluation on at least one standardised agent benchmark shows measurable improvement over LLM-only baseline.
MS3 | Memory architecture v1.0 — tri-store prototype operational | WP3 | M12 | Working, episodic, and semantic memory stores individually operational with basic memory controller. Evaluation on synthetic long-horizon tasks (more than 20 steps) shows coherence improvement over context-window-only baseline.
MS4 | Multi-agent protocol specification with convergence proofs | WP4 | M15 | Formal specification of task-delegation and negotiation protocols complete. Convergence proofs delivered for three or more core task classes. Simulation validation with five or more heterogeneous agents on synthetic coordination tasks confirmed.
MS5 | Demonstrator use cases defined and domain data secured | WP8 | M12 | Clinical use cases, manufacturing scenarios, and logistics corridors fully specified with acceptance criteria. Ethics approvals for clinical data access granted. Factory access agreement signed. Logistics data access terms agreed.
MS6 | Integrated MAESTRO framework v1.0 with tool orchestration layer | WP8 | M24 | All three research pillars and External Tool and API Orchestration Layer integrated into a single deployable framework. Tool registry populated with ten or more tools/APIs. End-to-end evaluation on cross-cutting benchmarks demonstrates all three capabilities and tool orchestration operating together.
MS7 | Demonstrator v1.0 — initial deployment in all three environments | WP8 | M30 | MAESTRO framework deployed in healthcare, manufacturing, and logistics demonstrator environments. Domain-specific adapters operational in each environment. Initial functional evaluation completed and gaps documented for framework refinement.
MS8 | Research pillar targets achieved | WP2 | M36 | OBJ-1 target met (forty or more percent plan success improvement). OBJ-2 target met (thirty or more percent coherence improvement). OBJ-3 target met (twenty-five or more percent joint task completion improvement). All results reproducible and documented.
MS9 | Demonstrator validation complete — all demonstrator environments | WP8 | M42 | Healthcare demonstrator validated on 500 clinical cases. Manufacturing demonstrator validated over 3-month operational period. Logistics transfer demonstrator validated across three international corridors. Tool orchestration layer validated with thirty or more tools/APIs registered. Cross-demonstrator evaluation report completed.
MS10 | Open-source release and platform contributions delivered | WP9 | M45 | MAESTRO framework released open-source on GitHub. Benchmark suite published on AI-on-demand platform. Documentation and tutorials available. EDIH dissemination initiated in three or more hubs.
MS11 | Project completion and final review | WP1 | M48 | All deliverables submitted. Final exploitation plans per partner delivered. TEF validation reports completed. Final review documentation compiled.

TABLE 3.1E — CRITICAL RISKS FOR IMPLEMENTATION

RISK-01 | Integrating symbolic planning formalisms with LLM-based reasoning may prove more difficult than anticipated, particularly for real-time performance in demonstrator environments. | Likelihood: Medium | Severity: High | WPs: WP2 | Mitigation: Modular architecture with well-defined interfaces between symbolic and neural components; LLM-only fallback mode when symbolic integration causes unacceptable latency; integration testing begins M6, not M18.
RISK-02 | Tri-store memory architecture may not scale to demonstrator interaction lengths without unacceptable latency or storage costs. | Likelihood: Medium | Severity: Medium | WPs: WP3 | Mitigation: Configurable retention policies designed from M1; scalability stress-testing on synthetic long-horizon tasks from M9; tiered storage with fast approximate retrieval for working memory and precise retrieval for episodic memory.
RISK-03 | Formal convergence proofs may be achievable only for restricted task classes, not for the full generality of real-world multi-agent scenarios. | Likelihood: Medium | Severity: Medium | WPs: WP4 | Mitigation: Task classification by coordination complexity at M3–M6; probabilistic convergence bounds for classes where deterministic proofs are infeasible; common demonstrator task patterns prioritised for formal proof.
RISK-04 | Access to de-identified clinical datasets may be delayed by ethics review processes or restricted in scope. | Likelihood: Medium | Severity: High | WPs: WP5 | Mitigation: Ethics applications submitted at M1; MIMIC-IV and eICU public datasets used for initial development; data abstraction layer enabling source switching without architectural changes.
RISK-05 | Factory access for the manufacturing demonstrator may be interrupted by production schedules, equipment maintenance, or external disruptions. | Likelihood: Low | Severity: Medium | WPs: WP6 | Mitigation: Formal access agreement secured before project start; 3-month validation scheduled with buffer windows; high-fidelity ISIA digital twin substitutes for physical access during development phases.
RISK-06 | ELI operational data may be subject to commercial confidentiality constraints restricting the scope or publishability of logistics demonstrator results. | Likelihood: Medium | Severity: Medium | WPs: WP7 | Mitigation: Data access and publication terms negotiated in the consortium agreement; anonymisation layer enabling publication of performance metrics without exposing proprietary data; supplemented with public logistics benchmarks.
RISK-07 | Fast pace of LLM development may produce new foundation models requiring re-evaluation of the MAESTRO value proposition. | Likelihood: High | Severity: Medium | WPs: WP2, WP3, WP4, WP8 | Mitigation: All MAESTRO components designed with model-agnostic interfaces supporting LLM substitution without architectural changes; annual baseline re-evaluation at M12, M24, M36; MAESTRO value resides in the planning, memory, and coordination architecture, not in any specific LLM.
RISK-08 | EU AI Act compliance obligations for high-risk AI systems (particularly the healthcare demonstrator) may impose additional development effort. | Likelihood: Medium | Severity: Medium | WPs: WP5, WP6, WP8 | Mitigation: Auditability and transparency mechanisms (audit trails, decision explanations) designed from project start; AI regulatory sandbox engagement where available; continuous monitoring of AI Act implementing regulations throughout the project.
RISK-09 | A key partner may reduce commitment or a principal investigator may depart, creating capability gaps. | Likelihood: Low | Severity: High | WPs: WP1 | Mitigation: Deputy PI appointments for each WP lead; consortium agreement commitment clauses and transition procedures; each research pillar has at least two partners with relevant expertise.
RISK-10 | Independently developed planning, memory, coordination, and tool orchestration modules may not integrate cleanly, requiring significant re-engineering. | Likelihood: Medium | Severity: High | WPs: WP2, WP3, WP4, WP8 | Mitigation: Shared data representations, API contracts, tool registry schemas, and integration interfaces defined M1–M3 and locked at MS1; integration checkpoints every 6 months; BAL assigned as integration engineer across all three pillars and the tool orchestration layer throughout the project.
RISK-11 | Diversity of external APIs across three domains (HL7 FHIR in healthcare, OPC-UA in manufacturing, logistics tracking and customs APIs) may require more adapter development effort than planned. | Likelihood: Medium | Severity: Medium | WPs: WP2, WP3, WP5, WP6, WP7, WP8 | Mitigation: Minimal common adapter interface defined M1–M3; critical-path APIs prioritised per demonstrator; existing open-source connector libraries used where available rather than building from scratch.

GANTT CHART AND TIMELINE NARRATIVE

The 48-month timeline is structured in five progressive phases ensuring technical readiness at each transition:

Phase 1 — Foundations (M1–M12): Research pillar WPs WP2 and WP3 begin at M1; WP4 begins at M3. MS1 (M3) locks shared integration architecture providing the common technical substrate for parallel development. The evaluation framework (T8-01, M6–M12) and benchmark specification (D8-01, M12) are established before demonstrators launch, ensuring all activities are measurable from inception consistent with CC-06. The DMP (D1-02, M6) and dissemination and exploitation plan (D9-01, M6) establish open science and communication governance from project start. All three research pillars reach evaluated intermediate prototypes by M12–M15: MS2 (planning engine v1.0, M12), MS3 (memory v1.0, M12), MS5 (demonstrator use cases and data access secured, M12), MS4 (multi-agent protocol specification with convergence proofs, M15).

Phase 2 — Integration and Demonstrator Launch (M12–M24): Demonstrators WP5, WP6, and WP7 launch at M12, consuming mature pillar outputs. D3-01 (memory design specification, M12) and D8-01 (evaluation framework, M12) complete. The first full integration of all research pillars and the External Tool and API Orchestration Layer is delivered at MS6 (M24), producing a deployable MAESTRO framework with ten or more tools/APIs registered.

Phase 3 — Demonstrator Deployment and Pillar Completion (M24–M36): All three demonstrators deploy MAESTRO instances in operational environments. Research pillar software prototypes complete: D3-02 (adaptive memory software, M30), D2-02 (planning engine software, M36), D4-02 (decentralised coordination software, M36). MS7 (M30) confirms initial deployment in all three environments. MS8 (M36) confirms quantitative research pillar targets achieved. Open-source release (D9-02, M36) enables early community uptake during the final validation phase.

Phase 4 — Demonstrator Validation (M36–M42): All three demonstrators complete full validation: healthcare on 500 clinical cases (WP5), manufacturing over a 3-month operational period (WP6), logistics across three international corridors (WP7). MS9 (M42) confirms successful validation and cross-demonstrator evaluation completion.

Phase 5 — Community Release and Project Closure (M42–M48): T8-03 (M42–M48) produces the final cross-demonstrator benchmark suite and AI-on-demand platform release. MS10 (M45) confirms publication and EDIH dissemination initiation. D8-03 (M48), D9-03 (M48), and MS11 (M48) mark project completion.

Critical path (source: gantt.json critical_path field): T2-01 (M1–M12) — T2-02 (M6–M24) — T2-03 (M18–M36) — T5-01 (M12–M18) — T5-02 (M18–M36) — T5-03 (M36–M42) — T8-03 (M42–M48). Nine tied critical path variants of equal structural length exist (through each of WP5, WP6, WP7 from each of WP2, WP3, WP4 as source node). The planning engine sequence anchors the representative critical path because T5-02 (HL7 FHIR integration and specialist sub-agent deployment) requires T2-03 (plan monitoring and self-correction) to be operationally mature. Delays in any research pillar propagate to demonstrator validation and ultimately to T8-03 (community benchmark release). This analysis motivates the intensive mitigations for RISK-01, RISK-02, RISK-03, and RISK-10 and underpins the decision to lock integration contracts at M3 before parallel development diverges.

INTER-WP DEPENDENCIES: The dependency structure flows from Layer 2 pillar WPs (WP2, WP3, WP4) to Layer 3 demonstrator WPs (WP5, WP6, WP7), then to WP8, then to WP9. WP1 supports all WPs throughout. The directed acyclic graph confirmed by cycle analysis in wp_structure.json (cycle_detected: false) contains no circular dependencies. All three demonstrators depend on all three research pillars, creating broad but structurally sound parallel-to-convergent flow. BAL serves as integration engineer across all three pillars and the tool orchestration layer throughout the project.

MANAGEMENT STRUCTURE AND GOVERNANCE

MAESTRO governance operates through three bodies composed exclusively of confirmed consortium partners:

General Assembly (GA): All eight partners. Biannual meetings. Highest strategic authority for major scope changes, consortium composition changes, approval of EC progress reports, and resolution of escalated disputes that cannot be resolved at PMC level. ATU (coordinator, Prof. Dr. Lena Hoffmann) chairs the GA.

Project Management Committee (PMC): All eight partners in their WP lead capacity. Quarterly meetings throughout the 48-month project. Responsible for operational execution monitoring, risk mitigation activation within approved parameters, technical issue resolution below scope change threshold, and inter-WP dependency coordination. ATU chairs and coordinates as part of WP1 T1-01.

Ethics and Quality Oversight Committee (EQOC): ATU (ethics compliance coordinator) and NIHS (healthcare ethics oversight officer). Minimum biannual meetings with immediate convening upon identification of material ethics issues. Manages the three triggered ethics categories: Category 8 (AI-based systems in safety-relevant domains), Category 4 (special category health data), and Category 2 (human participants in clinical validation context). Escalates to PMC and GA as required.

Escalation path: EQOC identifies and escalates ethics issues to PMC; PMC resolves operational issues or escalates strategic decisions to GA; GA is the final internal authority.

STAFF EFFORT AND RESOURCES

Detailed staff effort per participant per work package is provided in Table 3.1f consistent with the validated lump-sum budget for this project. All nine work packages have confirmed partner coverage in the validated budget response. Each WP lead holds primary effort responsibility for their respective WP, with contributions from named contributing partners as specified in Table 3.1b. Resource allocation reflects the technical intensity of the three research pillar WPs (WP2–WP4) during the first 30–36 months, the parallel demonstrator validation effort in WP5–WP7, and the cross-cutting integration effort of WP8 borne across all consortium partners. Specific person-month figures per participant per WP are recorded in Table 3.1f in the Part A budget tables, consistent with the validated budget. Subcontracting costs and major equipment costs are reported in Tables 3.1g and 3.1h respectively where applicable per the validated budget.

sub_sections.title: Capacity of participants and consortium as a whole
sub_sections.content:
CONSORTIUM AS A WHOLE

The MAESTRO consortium brings together eight European partners whose complementary expertise covers all three research pillars, all three demonstrator sectors required by the call (SR-07), and the horizontal evaluation, benchmarking, and dissemination capabilities needed to maximise scientific and societal impact. The consortium spans eight countries and four organisation types — higher education establishments (ATU, BIIS, ISIA, NIHS), research institutes (CERIA, FIIT), and private companies (BAL, ELI) — combining academic research depth with industrial deployment environments and commercial exploitation credibility. Each partner leads exactly one work package, ensuring unambiguous accountability and non-overlapping leadership responsibilities.

ATU — Alpenstadt Technical University (Germany, HES; Coordinator; WP1 and WP2 Lead)
ATU leads the project as coordinator and anchors the primary research pillar on neuro-symbolic planning and reasoning (WP2). The Chair of AI Foundations has pioneered work in neuro-symbolic AI, automated planning, and LLM-based reasoning systems — directly matched to WP2 objectives. Key personnel: Prof. Dr. Lena Hoffmann (Project Coordinator and Pillar 1 Scientific Lead, neuro-symbolic AI and automated planning, 15+ years of experience); Dr. Stefan Braun (Project Manager, 8 H2020/HE projects coordinated). ATU brings 128 A100 GPUs and national supercomputing centre access for large-scale LLM experiments. ATU has managed 12 EU-funded projects in the last 5 years with a track record of on-time, on-budget delivery of large collaborative RIAs, including prior contributions to the AI-on-demand platform. ATU participates in WP1 (lead), WP2 (lead), WP3, WP4, WP5, WP6, WP8, and WP9, ensuring deep cross-WP scientific coherence.

CERIA — Centre Européen de Recherche en Informatique Avancée (France, REC; WP4 Lead)
CERIA leads the Decentralised Multi-Agent Coordination pillar (WP4). The CERIA multi-agent systems team has extensive expertise in formal coordination protocols, distributed AI, and game-theoretic approaches to multi-agent cooperation, uniquely matched to the requirement for formally verified task-delegation and negotiation protocols with convergence guarantees. Key personnel: Dr. Claire Moreau (Pillar 3 Scientific Lead, multi-agent systems and formal coordination, 12+ years). CERIA brings a national grid computing infrastructure and multi-agent simulation testbed supporting 1,000+ concurrent agents. CERIA has participated in 50+ EU collaborative projects and holds an ERC Advanced Grant on formal methods for multi-agent systems. CERIA contributes to WP1, WP2, WP3, WP6, WP7, and WP8, reinforcing planning formalisms, supporting multi-actor coordination in the manufacturing and logistics demonstrators, and contributing to cross-cutting benchmarking.

BIIS — Breda Institute for Intelligent Systems (Netherlands, HES; WP3 Lead)
BIIS leads the Adaptive Memory Architecture pillar (WP3). BIIS hosts leading research groups in deep learning, information retrieval, and memory-augmented neural networks, with pioneering work in retrieval-augmented generation directly applicable to the unified tri-store memory design. Key personnel: Prof. Dr. Pieter Dekker (Pillar 2 Scientific Lead, memory architectures and neural retrieval, 10+ years). BIIS brings national supercomputer access and a dedicated ML cluster for RAG and memory system experiments. BIIS contributes to WP1, WP2, WP4, WP5, and WP8, ensuring memory–planning and memory–coordination interface coherence and supporting the clinical decision support demonstrator with long-horizon memory capabilities for specialist sub-agents.

ISIA — Istituto Superiore di Ingegneria Avanzata (Italy, HES; WP6 Lead)
ISIA leads the Manufacturing Demonstrator (WP6) and provides the pilot factory environment essential for advanced manufacturing sector validation (SR-07). The ISIA AI for Manufacturing lab has extensive experience in digital twins, smart factory systems, and AI-driven process optimisation in operational industrial settings, uniquely positioning ISIA to define the validation scenario and execute real-world testing under production conditions. Key personnel: Prof. Alessandro Ferretti (Manufacturing Demonstrator Lead, smart manufacturing and digital twins, 15+ years). ISIA brings an Industry 4.0 pilot factory with sensor-instrumented production lines and a digital twin simulation environment. ISIA has led 8 EU manufacturing-focused projects in H2020/HE and is an EIT Manufacturing partner. ISIA contributes to WP1, WP8, and WP9, bringing industrial domain perspective to the cross-cutting evaluation framework and exploitation activities.

NIHS — Nordic Institute of Health Sciences (Sweden, HES; WP5 Lead)
NIHS leads the Healthcare Demonstrator (WP5) and provides the clinical infrastructure, datasets, and validation pathways essential for healthcare Apply AI sector demonstration (SR-07). The NIHS Centre for Health Informatics conducts research on AI-assisted clinical decision support, medical knowledge representation, and clinical NLP. Key personnel: Prof. Karin Eriksson (Healthcare Demonstrator Lead, clinical informatics and AI-assisted diagnostics, 12+ years). NIHS brings a de-identified clinical data repository of over 3 million ethics-approved patient records and a clinical simulation environment for AI validation. NIHS holds responsibility for healthcare ethics oversight (GDPR compliance for special category health data, Categories 2, 4, and 8 management) and brings HL7 FHIR integration expertise that no other consortium partner possesses. NIHS contributes to WP1 and WP8, providing clinical domain knowledge to the cross-cutting evaluation framework and ensuring the healthcare demonstrator evaluation dataset meets standards for scientific reproducibility.

ELI — EuroLog International AG (Switzerland, PRC; WP7 Lead)
ELI leads the Logistics Transfer Demonstrator (WP7) and provides the real-world operational environments needed for cross-sector generalisation validation beyond the primary call sectors. ELI is a major European logistics company operating in over 80 countries; its Innovation Lab develops AI-powered logistics optimisation and autonomous planning systems. Key personnel: Dr. Markus Steiner (Logistics Demonstrator Lead, supply chain AI and logistics optimisation, 10+ years). ELI brings anonymised operational logistics data across 30+ countries and a supply chain simulation environment with multi-actor coordination. ELI provides the commercial exploitation pathway into the logistics sector, demonstrating that MAESTRO results have a direct route to industrial adoption at scale. Switzerland is an associated country eligible for Horizon Europe participation per CC-08. ELI contributes to WP1, WP8, and WP9.

BAL — Boreal AI Labs Oy (Finland, PRC; WP9 Lead)
BAL leads Dissemination, Exploitation and Communication (WP9) and serves as the LLM engineering and platform integration specialist across all research pillar and demonstrator WPs. BAL is a leading European private AI lab specialising in custom LLM development, fine-tuning, and production-grade AI deployment, with a track record in European open-source LLM development. Key personnel: Dr. Antti Korhonen (LLM Engineering Lead, LLM development and AI productisation, 8+ years). BAL brings EuroHPC pre-exascale supercomputer access and production AI deployment infrastructure (Kubernetes, GPU clusters). BAL's open-source community management experience directly supports the AI-on-demand platform mandate (SR-10, CC-05) and the open-source software release (D9-02). BAL contributes to WP1, WP2, WP3, WP5, WP6, WP7, and WP8, providing the LLM engineering bridge between research pillar outputs and their production-grade deployment across all three demonstrators — a role that no purely academic partner in the consortium is positioned to fulfil.

FIIT — Fundación Ibérica de Innovación Tecnológica (Spain, REC; WP8 Lead)
FIIT leads Integration, Evaluation and Benchmarking (WP8) and brings AI evaluation methodology, benchmarking, and technology transfer expertise as an independent evaluator separate from all three research pillar leads. The FIIT AI and Data Analytics division leads AI-on-demand platform contributions and EDIH network coordination. Key personnel: Dr. Isabel Navarro (Evaluation and Benchmarking Lead, AI evaluation and technology transfer, 10+ years). FIIT brings AI Testing and Experiment Facility (TEF) access per CC-07 and EDIH network connections across 6 EU member states for post-project dissemination. FIIT has prior contributions to the AI-on-demand platform from H2020 projects. FIIT contributes to WP1, WP5, WP6, WP7, and WP9, ensuring cross-demonstrator evaluation is rigorous, reproducible, and community-ready for benchmark adoption.

CONSORTIUM COMPLEMENTARITY AND VALUE CHAIN COVERAGE

The consortium covers the full value chain from foundational research (ATU, CERIA, BIIS) through applied sector validation in real operational environments (NIHS, ISIA, ELI) to evaluation, benchmarking, and open-source community deployment (FIIT, BAL). The three research universities and institutes (ATU, CERIA, BIIS) provide the formal methods, machine learning, and multi-agent systems depth required to advance from TRL 2 to TRL 5 per CC-01 across the three research pillars. The sector-specific partners with operational infrastructure (ISIA: pilot factory; NIHS: clinical data repository and validation pathways; ELI: international logistics corridors) provide the validated real-world environments that distinguish this proposal from purely laboratory-scale research. BAL provides the production-grade LLM engineering and deployment capability that bridges research outputs to the demonstrator deployment environments, a role not covered by any other partner. FIIT provides evaluation independence — as WP8 lead, FIIT evaluates outputs from all three research pillar WPs in which it is not a lead — and the TEF access required by CC-07. The two private companies (BAL, ELI) ensure commercial exploitation perspectives and realistic deployment experience are embedded throughout the project, not added at the end. Each of the eight partners leads exactly one work package and has adequate resources confirmed in the validated budget to fulfil their WP leadership responsibilities. The scientific specialisms across the eight partners are non-overlapping at the primary capability level, and each partner's expertise is uniquely matched to their WP leadership role as evidenced in Tier 3 capabilities.json, partners.json, and roles.json.
